name: Ometochtli
description: Ometochtli, meaning 'Two Rabbit' in Nahuatl, is not a single deity but rather a collective name or title for a group of gods associated with pulque, the fermented alcoholic beverage made from agave. These gods were central to rituals and celebrations involving pulque, representing intoxication, fertility, and the loosening of social constraints. Each individual Ometochtli god presided over specific aspects of pulque consumption and its associated behaviors, such as revelry, divination, and artistic inspiration. They were believed to have the power to influence both the positive and negative effects of drinking, bringing either joy and creativity or chaos and destruction. Ometochtli's influence extended beyond mere inebriation, encompassing themes of duality, transformation, and the cyclical nature of life. They were often depicted with rabbit imagery, symbolizing fertility and abundance, and were venerated through offerings of pulque and ritualistic dances. Their worship highlighted the complex relationship the Aztecs had with alcohol, recognizing its potential for both spiritual enlightenment and social disruption. The Ometochtli pantheon served as a reminder of the delicate balance between control and abandon, and the importance of moderation in all things. The number 'two' in their name signifies duality and the contrasting aspects of the human condition that pulque could reveal.
subtitle: Gods of Pulque, Intoxication, and Revelry

keyMyths.title: Origin of Pulque
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel, the goddess of agave, is often associated with the Ometochtli. Her cultivation of the agave plant led to the creation of pulque, the sacred beverage.

keyMyths.title: The Centzon Totochtin
keyMyths.description: The Centzon Totochtin are the 400 rabbit gods of pulque, representing various aspects of intoxication and its effects on individuals. They are collectively known as Ometochtli.

keyMyths.title: Rituals and Offerings
keyMyths.description: Ometochtli were venerated through rituals involving the consumption of pulque, music, dance, and offerings of food and drink. These ceremonies aimed to invoke their blessings and ensure a bountiful harvest.
